author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
title: Names Do Not Imply Corporeity.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: You also say, that “names cease to be given, when form is not distinguished; and that, where there is no designation of persons, there is no giving of names.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Your aim is to prove that Abraham’s soul was corporeal, inasmuch as he could be addressed as “Father Abraham.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, we have already said, that there is form even where there is no body.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If, however, you think that where there are not bodies there is no assigning of names, I must beg of you to count the names which occur in this passage of Scripture, “But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance,” and tell me whether you do not recognise the very things of which these are the names; or whether you recognise them so as to descry some outlines of bodies.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Come, tell me, to mention only love, for instance, what are its members, its figure, its colour?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For if you are not yourself empty-headed, these appurtenances cannot possibly be regarded by you as an empty thing.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Then you go on to say: “The look and form must, of course, be corporeal of him whose help is implored.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Well, let men hear what you say; and let no one implore God’s help, because no one can possibly see anything corporeal in Him.
